vulnerabilities.title: PEMCertChainLib notBefore Date Tag Validation Logic Bug Accepts Invalid Certificate Tags
vulnerabilities.summary: The notBefore date tag validation in PEMCertChainLib.decodeCert contains a logic error. The condition `(notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag == 0x18)` simplifies to `(notBeforeTag == 0x18)`. This means: (1) Valid GeneralizedTime (0x18) certificates are REJECTED, causing DoS for legitimate attestations. (2) Invalid/arbitrary tags (e.g., 0x05, 0x30) for notBefore are ACCEPTED, allowing certificates with garbage notBefore timestamps to pass validation. X509DateUtils.toTimestamp would parse arbitrar

description.desc: Condition `(notBeforeTag != 0x17 && notBeforeTag == 0x18)` is logically equivalent to `(notBeforeTag == 0x18)`, rejecting valid GeneralizedTime while accepting arbitrary invalid tags

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL for attestation integrity. The bug has two consequences: (1) Certificates using GeneralizedTime for notBefore are incorrectly rejected (DoS for valid attestations). (2) Certificates with invalid/arbitrary notBefore tags are accepted, potentially allowing garbage timestamps that make expired certificates appear valid. While exploitation for the security bypass requires crafting a certificate with valid signatures (significant difficulty), the code defect is clear and unambiguous. The notAfter check on the same line proves the developer's intent.

vulnerabilities.title: Certificate Chain Verification Skips Root CA Expiry and Self-Signature Validation
vulnerabilities.summary: In _verifyCertChain, for a 3-cert chain [PCK, Intermediate, Root], the loop breaks at i=1 when the issuer (root) pubkey hash matches ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH. This means the root certificate (certs[2]) is NEVER validated for expiry (notBefore/notAfter) or self-signature. An expired root CA certificate would be accepted as valid.

description.desc: Loop breaks when issuerPubKeyHash == ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH at i=1, never reaching i=2 to validate root cert expiry

vulnerabilities.impact: The defect allows attestation to succeed with an expired root CA certificate. While the practical impact is limited by the Intel SGX Root CA's long validity period, the code violates the X.509 certificate chain validation invariant that ALL certificates must be within their validity period. Additionally, the root cert's self-signature is never verified, meaning a certificate with the correct public key but a forged or corrupt TBS section could be accepted. This weakens the security guarantees of the attestation system.

vulnerabilities.title: AutomataDcapV3Attestation Has No Ownership Transfer Mechanism
vulnerabilities.summary: AutomataDcapV3Attestation uses a custom owner variable set in the constructor with no transfer or renounce mechanism. Unlike EssentialContract-derived contracts that use Ownable2Step, this contract provides no way to change the owner. If the owner key is lost, all admin functions become permanently inaccessible. If compromised, the attacker has irrevocable control.

description.desc: owner = msg.sender in constructor; no transferOwnership function exists

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The code defect is real — the contract lacks a standard ownership transfer mechanism that all other admin contracts in the codebase have. Impact is primarily operational: key loss permanently freezes configuration, key compromise grants irrevocable control. The architectural workaround via address manager replacement provides a recovery path but at significant operational cost (new deployment, full reconfiguration). The defect directly violates the security pattern established by other contracts in the system.

vulnerabilities.title: PEMCertChainLib _findTcb Array Out-of-Bounds When PCESVN Is Not Last SVN Entry
vulnerabilities.summary: In _findTcb, cpusvns array has size SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE (16) but the loop iterates SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE + 1 (17) times. When the PCESVN_OID is encountered, the value is stored in pcesvn (not cpusvns). If PCESVN_OID is NOT the last entry in the DER sequence, the 17th iteration (i=16) would attempt cpusvns[16], causing an out-of-bounds revert in Solidity 0.8.x. Additionally, if PCESVN appears mid-sequence, cpusvns has a zero-valued gap at that index.

description.desc: cpusvns = new uint256[](16); loop 0..16 (17 iterations); cpusvns[i] written when i=16 and not PCESVN causes OOB

vulnerabilities.impact: The defect is real but currently mitigated by Intel's consistent certificate format placing PCESVN last. If the format changes or an unexpected certificate is encountered: (1) OOB revert causes DoS for attestation verification, (2) zero-gap in cpusvns causes incorrect TCB level comparisons potentially accepting or rejecting attestations incorrectly. Since the contract is non-upgradeable, a format change would require redeployment.

vulnerabilities.title: MerkleClaimable.setConfig() lacks sanity checks (start/end/root), enabling accidental or malicious permanent claim lockout
vulnerabilities.summary: MerkleClaimable._setConfig directly assigns claimStart/claimEnd/merkleRoot without validating ordering (claimStart < claimEnd), nonzero root, or future/valid ranges. A misconfiguration can make ongoingClaim always revert (e.g., claimStart > claimEnd, claimEnd=0, merkleRoot=0), halting claims/withdrawals that depend on claims.

description.desc: _setConfig assigns values with no validation; ongoingClaim reverts if any are zero or time window invalid

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. The defect is in input validation for an admin-only function, but: (1) the deployment pattern explicitly requires a setConfig call, making misconfiguration a real operational risk; (2) all eligible airdrop recipients are affected simultaneously; (3) time-bounded claims mean even temporary misconfiguration can cause permanent loss for some users; (4) no event is emitted with the new config values for monitoring. The owner CAN fix it by calling setConfig again, but the damage during the invalid window may be irrecoverable for users whose claim deadlines pass.

vulnerabilities.title: SigVerifyLib.verifyRS256Signature/verifyRS1Signature assumes 3-byte exponent, enabling signature verification failures or unexpected acceptance if exponent length differs
vulnerabilities.summary: SigVerifyLib slices the RSA public key as exponent=publicKey.substring(0,3) and modulus=remaining. This hard-codes exponent length to 3 bytes (commonly 0x010001), but X.509 RSA exponents can be encoded with different lengths. If a certificate chain includes a different exponent length, verification will either revert (substring bounds) or verify against an incorrectly parsed exponent/modulus pair, potentially causing false negatives (DoS) or, in worst case, mismatched verification behavior.

description.desc: bytes memory exponent = publicKey.substring(0, 3); bytes memory modulus = publicKey.substring(3, publicKey.length - 3);

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. The defect is a real code bug: hard-coded assumption about RSA exponent length with no validation. Impact depends on usage: (1) The current DCAP attestation flow primarily uses ES256, limiting immediate impact; (2) The public RSA functions would silently fail for non-65537 exponent keys; (3) Any future use of the RSA path with non-standard keys would be broken; (4) The ISigVerifyLib interface design has an inherent ambiguity (no exponent length field) that makes this defect easy to trigger.

vulnerabilities.title: TimelockTokenPool Withdrawal Signature Lacks Domain Separation - Cross-Chain Replay
vulnerabilities.summary: The withdraw(address,bytes) function recovers the recipient from a signature over keccak256(abi.encodePacked('Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ', _to)). This hash includes no chain ID, contract address, nonce, or expiry. A valid signature observed on-chain on one chain can be replayed on any other chain where the same recipient has a grant in another TimelockTokenPool instance.

description.desc: keccak256(abi.encodePacked('Withdraw unlocked Taiko token to: ', _to)) has no chainid, no address(this), no nonce

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. The signature scheme violates standard domain-separation best practices (EIP-712, SWC-121). If the same recipient has grants across multiple TimelockTokenPool instances (different chains or different role pools on the same chain), any signature for one instance is valid for all. This enables forced withdrawals to potentially uncontrolled addresses, token theft if _to is an address controlled differently across chains, and forced timing of withdrawals the recipient did not intend.

vulnerabilities.title: ERC20Airdrop2 withdraw() Allows Anyone to Force Token Delivery to Any User
vulnerabilities.summary: ERC20Airdrop2.withdraw(address user) is externally callable by anyone with no access control or nonReentrant guard. Any third party can trigger token withdrawal for any user at any time during the withdrawal window, forcing the user to receive tokens at a time they did not choose.

description.desc: function withdraw(address user) external ongoingWithdrawals — no msg.sender check, no nonReentrant

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. Tokens flow to the correct user (no fund theft), but the user loses control over withdrawal timing. In jurisdictions where token receipt is a taxable event, this could cause real financial harm. The missing nonReentrant is a secondary concern since state ordering prevents double-spend, but represents a missing defense-in-depth measure that could become exploitable if the token has complex callback mechanisms.

vulnerabilities.title: ERC20Airdrop2 Permanent Fund Lock if withdrawalWindow Initialized to Zero
vulnerabilities.summary: ERC20Airdrop2.init does not validate that _withdrawalWindow > 0. If initialized with _withdrawalWindow = 0, the getBalance function divides by withdrawalWindow, causing a division-by-zero revert. All calls to withdraw() would permanently revert, locking all claimed airdrop tokens forever.

description.desc: No validation _withdrawalWindow > 0 in init; getBalance divides by withdrawalWindow

vulnerabilities.impact: If deployed with withdrawalWindow=0, the entire airdrop allocation becomes permanently unrecoverable through normal contract operations. All users who claim tokens will have their claimedAmount recorded but will never be able to withdraw. This constitutes a complete loss of the airdrop for all participants. The contract is UUPS-upgradeable, so the owner could deploy a new implementation to fix the issue, but this requires significant effort and governance action.

vulnerabilities.title: Cross-Chain Ownership Execution Always Reverts for Protected Functions
vulnerabilities.summary: The `CrossChainOwned` contract uses `address(this).call(txdata)` to execute cross-chain messages. This low-level call changes `msg.sender` to `address(this)`. However, administrative functions like `pause`, `withdraw`, and `setConfigAndExcess` are protected by `onlyOwner` or `onlyFromOwnerOrNamed`, which strictly require `msg.sender` to be the L1 owner address. As a result, all cross-chain administrative calls will revert, permanently locking the protocol's governance capabilities on L2.

description.desc: address(this).call(txdata) changes msg.sender to address(this), failing onlyOwner checks
Affected function: pause()

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL impact: (1) EIP-1559 gas parameters (gasExcess, basefee configuration) cannot be updated via cross-chain governance. (2) The contract cannot be paused or unpaused via cross-chain governance. (3) ETH and ERC20 tokens held by TaikoL2 cannot be withdrawn via cross-chain governance (unless a separate 'withdrawer' role is configured locally). (4) The UUPS proxy cannot be upgraded via cross-chain governance. This effectively means the L2 protocol governance is non-functional for its primary intended administrative path.

vulnerabilities.title: Airdrop Double-Dipping via Merkle Root Updates
vulnerabilities.summary: The `MerkleClaimable` contract allows the owner to update the Merkle root via `setConfig`. The claim hash is generated using `keccak256(abi.encode("CLAIM_TAIKO_AIRDROP", data))`, where `data` includes the user's allocation amount. If the owner updates the root to increase a user's allocation, the new hash will not be marked as claimed, allowing the user to claim the new amount IN ADDITION to their previously claimed amount, draining the airdrop vault.

description.desc: Claim hash includes amount, allowing re-claiming if amount changes in new Merkle root

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. Any merkle root update where existing users have different allocation amounts enables double-claiming. For ERC20Airdrop, tokens are transferred directly on each claim. For ERC20Airdrop2, the claimedAmount accumulates, giving users more withdrawal allowance. The total value at risk is the sum of all duplicated allocations across both the old and new merkle trees. In the worst case, the vault could be drained if the total double-claimed amount exceeds vault balance.

vulnerabilities.title: L2 Chain Halt via Uninitialized EIP-1559 Config Upgrade
vulnerabilities.summary: If the L2 anchor contract is upgraded to `TaikoL2EIP1559Configurable` but `setConfigAndExcess` is not called atomically, `customConfig` remains zero. `getConfig()` will return zeros, causing `_calc1559BaseFee` to pass an `_adjustmentFactor` of 0 to `Lib1559Math.basefee`, which reverts. This causes the `anchor` transaction to fail, permanently halting the L2 chain since no new blocks can be proposed.

description.desc: Returns uninitialized customConfig (zeros) causing division by zero in Lib1559Math
Affected function: _calc1559BaseFee()

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. Complete and permanent L2 chain halt affecting all users and the entire TVL of the network. No in-protocol recovery mechanism exists since the anchor transaction is mandatory for block production and the fix transaction cannot be included in any block. The defect is in the code itself: getConfig() lacks a fallback to safe default values when customConfig is uninitialized.

vulnerabilities.title: ERC721Airdrop claim loop can be weaponized for gas-exhaustion DoS if merkle leaf allows large tokenIds arrays
vulnerabilities.summary: ERC721Airdrop.claim verifies a merkle leaf of (user, tokenIds) and then loops over tokenIds to transfer NFTs. If the merkle tree includes a leaf with a very large tokenIds array (by mistake or as part of a bulk grant), the claim may become uncallable due to block gas limits, effectively locking those NFTs in the vault for that recipient. Attackers cannot change the tree, but they can exploit operational mistakes by selecting a leaf with unbounded array size and demonstrating permanent non-claima

description.desc: for (uint256 i; i < tokenIds.length; ++i) IERC721(token).safeTransferFrom(vault, user, tokenIds[i]);

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM impact. If a merkle leaf with a sufficiently large tokenIds array exists, the associated NFTs are permanently locked in the vault for that recipient. Recovery requires admin intervention (manual transfer from vault or contract upgrade with new merkle root). The severity is bounded by the fact that this requires an operational mistake in merkle tree construction, but the code provides zero safeguards against it.

vulnerabilities.title: PEMCertChainLib splitCertificateChain can desync parsing boundaries, enabling malformed PEM chains that pass split but decode into unexpected DER segments
vulnerabilities.summary: splitCertificateChain() repeatedly calls _removeHeadersAndFooters() on progressively sliced strings using an 'index += increment' cursor. _removeHeadersAndFooters finds the first HEADER/FOOTER occurrences via indexOf without validating ordering or ensuring the footer corresponds to the matching header (e.g., nested headers, extra footers, or injected header/footer sequences). This may allow a crafted PEM chain string to cause extraction of attacker-chosen base64 fragments as 'certs[i]' while ski

description.desc: splitCertificateChain uses index cursor + _removeHeadersAndFooters with indexOf(HEADER/FOOTER) on each slice; no check footer after header or unique pairing

vulnerabilities.impact: The confirmed defect enables DoS attacks against the attestation verification pipeline. An attacker can craft PEM chains that pass the initial splitCertificateChain parsing (returns success=true) but produce corrupted DER data that causes expensive reverts during ASN.1 parsing or certificate decoding. While the cryptographic verification (signature checks against ROOTCA_PUBKEY_HASH) prevents actual acceptance of fraudulent attestations, the gas griefing impact is real. The worst case involves pathological PEM strings that maximize gas consumption before reverting, affecting all SGX/DCAP attestation verification calls.

vulnerabilities.title: Triangular Double-Counting of L1 Block Issuance Drains EIP-1559 Gas Excess
vulnerabilities.summary: The EIP-1559 base fee calculation incorrectly accumulates L1 block issuance due to delayed updates of `lastSyncedBlock`. Because `lastSyncedBlock` is only updated every `BLOCK_SYNC_THRESHOLD` blocks, `numL1Blocks` grows linearly (1, 2, 3...) for each L2 block anchored within the threshold window. This causes the gas issuance subtraction to be applied triangularly (e.g., 1+2+3+4+5+6 = 21 times the target instead of 6), rapidly draining `gasExcess` to 1 and permanently breaking the dynamic base fe

description.desc: numL1Blocks = _l1BlockId - lastSyncedBlock; issuance = numL1Blocks * gasTarget; excess -= issuance; but lastSyncedBlock is not updated every block

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL - The L2 EIP-1559 fee market is fundamentally broken. During normal operation, gasExcess rapidly drains to 1, making the base fee permanently 1 wei. This allows: (1) unlimited cheap spam transactions on L2, (2) complete loss of sequencer fee revenue, (3) network congestion and degraded UX for all users. The defect manifests automatically without any attacker action - it's a mathematical error in the core economic mechanism.

vulnerabilities.title: Precision Loss in Vesting Cost Calculation Allows Fractional Token Theft
vulnerabilities.summary: In `TimelockTokenPool`, the cost required to withdraw tokens is calculated as `costToWithdraw = (amountUnlocked / 1e18) * costPerToken - costPaid`. Because the division by `1e18` occurs before multiplication, the fractional part of `amountUnlocked` (up to `0.999... * 1e18` wei) is truncated. A user can withdraw their entire `amountUnlocked` (including the fractional part) but will only pay for the integer part. At the end of the vesting period, the user permanently avoids paying `costPerToken` f

description.desc: uint128 _amountUnlocked = amountUnlocked / 1e18; costToWithdraw = _amountUnlocked * r.grant.costPerToken - r.costPaid;

vulnerabilities.impact: Each recipient avoids up to ~1 * costPerToken in payment. With N recipients, total protocol loss is up to N * costPerToken. If costPerToken represents a stablecoin value (e.g., USDC at 6 decimals), each user saves up to ~$1 equivalent. For hundreds of grant recipients, total loss could be hundreds of dollars. While the per-user impact is small, this is a systematic accounting error that accumulates across all recipients. The code comment explicitly acknowledges this trade-off, indicating it was a conscious design choice for storage efficiency, but it still constitutes a violation of the economic invariant that cost paid should be proportional to tokens received.

vulnerabilities.title: Extending claimEnd Causes Underflow and Locks Airdrop Withdrawals
vulnerabilities.summary: In `ERC20Airdrop2`, the `timeBasedAllowance` is calculated relative to `claimEnd`. If the admin uses `setConfig` to extend `claimEnd` to a later date, the calculated `timeBasedAllowance` for all users will instantly decrease. If a user has already withdrawn tokens such that `withdrawnAmount[user] > new_timeBasedAllowance`, the calculation `withdrawableAmount = timeBasedAllowance - withdrawnAmount[user]` will revert due to arithmetic underflow. This completely breaks `getBalance` and `withdraw` f

description.desc: withdrawableAmount = timeBasedAllowance - withdrawnAmount[user]; // underflows if timeBasedAllowance decreases

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. Users who have partially withdrawn are locked out of their remaining funds for a duration proportional to how much claimEnd was extended. For example, if claimEnd is extended by 30 days and a user had withdrawn 50% of their allocation, they would be locked out for approximately 30 days until timeBasedAllowance catches back up. The funds are not permanently lost but are temporarily inaccessible, causing DoS. Frontend integrations relying on getBalance() not reverting would also break.

vulnerabilities.title: QE Report TCB Level Selection Depends on Array Ordering - Wrong Status May Be Selected
vulnerabilities.summary: In _verifyQEReportWithIdentity, the function iterates through enclaveId.tcbLevels and selects the FIRST entry where tcb.tcb.isvsvn <= quoteEnclaveReport.isvSvn. This first-match semantic means if tcbLevels are not sorted in strictly descending order of isvsvn, the function may select a less restrictive (or more restrictive) TCB status than intended. For example, if a revoked level appears before a valid level with a lower SVN threshold, the revoked status would be returned even for legitimate qu

description.desc: First-match loop: for (uint256 i; i < enclaveId.tcbLevels.length; ++i) { if (tcb.tcb.isvsvn <= quoteEnclaveReport.isvSvn) { tcbFound = true; status = tcb.tcbStatus; break; } }

vulnerabilities.impact: If tcbLevels are misconfigured in non-descending order: (1) Valid SGX attestations could be rejected (DoS on provers), disrupting L2 block proving. (2) In the reverse ordering scenario, insecure attestations from revoked enclaves could be accepted, compromising SGX proof integrity. The impact severity depends on the specific misconfiguration and the role of SGX attestation in the Taiko protocol's security model.

vulnerabilities.title: changeBridgedToken Deletes Wrong Mapping Key Leaving Stale Canonical Reference
vulnerabilities.summary: In ERC20Vault.changeBridgedToken, the code executes `delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew]` which is a no-op since _btokenNew was verified to have no existing mapping (bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew].addr == address(0)). The intended deletion should be `delete bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]` to clean up the old bridged token's canonical mapping. After migration, btokenOld_ retains its bridgedToCanonical mapping despite being blacklisted.

description.desc: delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew] is no-op; should be delete bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]

vulnerabilities.impact: This is a confirmed code defect (wrong variable in delete statement). The immediate practical impact is limited because the blacklist prevents direct use of btokenOld_ via sendToken. However: (1) Storage inconsistency persists permanently unless manually corrected via upgrade. (2) The bridgedToCanonical ↔ canonicalToBridged bijection invariant is broken. (3) Any future code path or upgrade that reads bridgedToCanonical without checking btokenBlacklist could be affected. (4) The stale entry wastes storage and could mislead off-chain systems. This is a real bug regardless of current exploitability.

vulnerabilities.title: Bridge Message with Short Data Bypasses Selector Check Allowing Arbitrary Fallback Invocation
vulnerabilities.summary: In _invokeMessageCall, the selector restriction only applies when _message.data.length >= 4 AND _message.to.isContract(). If data.length is 1-3 bytes and the target is a contract, the call proceeds to excessivelySafeCall with the short data. This triggers the target contract's fallback function with arbitrary short payloads while carrying _message.value in ETH and having bridge context active.

description.desc: _message.data.length >= 4 check means <4 bytes bypass selector restriction entirely

vulnerabilities.impact: The defect is a real code logic gap - the selector restriction is designed to limit contract calls to only onMessageInvocation, but data of 1-3 bytes bypasses this entirely. The practical impact depends on whether any deployed contracts have fallback functions that read Bridge.context() for authorization. Currently, known protocol contracts (vaults, bridged tokens) use onMessageInvocation for bridge interactions and do not have exploitable fallback functions. However, the defect represents an incomplete security boundary that could become exploitable if new contracts are deployed that rely on the bridge context in their fallback functions. The attacker can also send ETH value to the target during this call.

vulnerabilities.title: Relayer Gas Griefing Fee Theft via Insufficient Gas in processMessage
vulnerabilities.summary: A malicious relayer can call processMessage with deliberately insufficient gas. The EVM's 63/64 rule causes excessivelySafeCall to forward less gas than _message.gasLimit to the target, causing the invocation to fail. The message is marked RETRIABLE and the relayer pockets the entire fee, while the user must pay gas again to retry with no fee incentive for retriers.

description.desc: gasLimit = msg.sender == _message.destOwner ? gasleft() : _message.gasLimit; No check that gasleft() >= _message.gasLimit before forwarding to excessivelySafeCall

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH impact. Every bridge message with a non-zero fee on L2 is vulnerable. Users lose their fee and must manually retry (retryMessage has no fee incentive for third parties). The attack is permissionless, requires no special privileges, and is profitable for any message where fee > attacker's gas cost. This undermines the entire relayer incentive mechanism of the bridge.

vulnerabilities.title: Bridge staticcall Prevents SignalService Proof Caching, Causing Valid Proofs to Revert
vulnerabilities.summary: Bridge._proveSignalReceived uses staticcall to invoke SignalService.proveSignalReceived. However, proveSignalReceived may call _syncChainData (which performs SSTORE) when CacheOption is non-zero. SSTORE in a staticcall context causes the entire staticcall to revert, making the proof appear invalid. This means any proof submitted with cache options through Bridge will be silently rejected.

description.desc: _signalService.staticcall(data) where data encodes proveSignalReceived which may SSTORE via _cacheChainData
Affected function: proveSignalReceived

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect makes the caching feature of SignalService completely unusable through Bridge interactions (processMessage, recallMessage, proveMessageFailed, proveMessageReceived). Relayers who attempt to use caching will have their proofs silently rejected. Messages can still be processed using CACHE_NOTHING, so this is not a complete DoS. However: (1) the caching optimization intended to reduce future proof costs cannot be leveraged through Bridge, (2) relayers unaware of this limitation waste gas on failed transactions, (3) there's a mismatch between the SignalService API (which offers caching) and the Bridge's usage of it.

vulnerabilities.title: Permanent Lock of Bridged Assets via Push-over-Pull ETH Refund DoS
vulnerabilities.summary: The Bridge and Vault contracts use a push-over-pull pattern for ETH refunds and asset recalls. If the refund recipient (refundTo) or source owner is a smart contract that reverts on receiving ETH, the entire transaction reverts, permanently locking the bridged assets in the NEW state.

description.desc: refundTo.sendEther(_message.fee + refundAmount) pushes ETH and reverts on failure
Affected function: onMessageRecalled

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. Users who bridge assets through smart contract wallets or specify refundTo as contracts that cannot receive ETH (or revert on empty calls) will permanently lose their bridged assets. The tokens are locked in the source vault with no recovery mechanism. While most popular smart contract wallets (like Gnosis Safe) typically have fallback functions, custom contracts, some proxy implementations, and certain DeFi protocol contracts may lack them. The vulnerability also affects onMessageRecalled in all vault contracts where srcOwner.sendEther is used.

vulnerabilities.title: Global Reentrancy Lock Prevents Cross-Chain Contract Composability
vulnerabilities.summary: Both Bridge.sendMessage and Bridge.processMessage use the nonReentrant modifier, which relies on a single contract-level lock. When processMessage invokes a target contract, the lock is held. If the target contract attempts to send a message back (e.g., returning excess funds or acknowledging receipt) by calling Bridge.sendMessage, the call will revert with REENTRANT_CALL.

description.desc: nonReentrant modifier uses a single contract-level lock (_REENTRY_SLOT)
Affected function: sendMessage

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity for composability. Any contract on the destination chain that automatically triggers Bridge.sendMessage upon receiving a cross-chain message will have its invocation fail. This prevents: (1) cross-chain ping-pong patterns, (2) atomic cross-chain swap protocols, (3) automated yield harvesting with return messages, (4) any DeFi protocol that needs to acknowledge or respond to cross-chain messages atomically. The impact is permanent and affects all chains where the Bridge is deployed.

vulnerabilities.title: USDCAdapter Burn Requires Direct User Allowance Causing Bridging Reverts
vulnerabilities.summary: When bridging USDC back to the canonical chain, ERC20Vault.sendToken calls BridgedERC20(token).burn(msg.sender, amount). For the USDCAdapter, _burnToken uses usdc.transferFrom(_from, address(this), _amount). This requires the user to have approved the USDCAdapter contract directly, rather than the ERC20Vault.

description.desc: usdc.transferFrom(_from, address(this), _amount) requires direct allowance to adapter
Affected function: sendToken

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. All USDC bridging-back operations fail if users follow the standard approval flow. This affects every user attempting to bridge USDC from L2 to L1 via the standard ERC20Vault.sendToken path. The workaround exists (approve USDCAdapter directly), but it breaks the standard UX pattern and requires special-case handling in frontends and integrating contracts.

vulnerabilities.title: Banning Vault Address Causes Permanent Loss of In-Flight Bridged Tokens
vulnerabilities.summary: If the watchdog bans an address (e.g., ERC20Vault) during an emergency, processMessage immediately marks any in-flight messages to that address as DONE and refunds the ETH value. However, the token transfer payload is ignored. Because the status is DONE (not FAILED), the users cannot recall their tokens on the source chain.

description.desc: addressBanned[_message.to] marks message DONE without executing payload

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. During an emergency ban of a vault address, all in-flight token bridge messages are permanently destroyed. Tokens locked/burned on the source chain cannot be recovered. The impact scales with the number of in-flight messages and their values at the time of the ban. This is particularly dangerous because banning is an emergency response mechanism, so it likely coincides with high-volume/high-value situations. The defect turns a protective measure into a fund-destroying one.

vulnerabilities.title: Negative Rebasing Tokens Cause Vault Insolvency and Loss of Funds
vulnerabilities.summary: If a canonical token is a negative rebasing token, its balance decreases over time. The ERC20Vault tracks user deposits based on the initial balance change but does not account for subsequent rebases. Over time, the vault's actual balance will fall below the total supply of bridged tokens.

description.desc: balanceChange_ = t.balanceOf(address(this)) - _balance captures initial deposit but ignores future rebases

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. If a negative rebasing token is bridged, the vault becomes progressively insolvent. Late withdrawers lose up to 100% of their bridged assets. The impact scales with the total value bridged and the magnitude/frequency of negative rebases. While the precondition requires a rebasing token to be bridged (which may not happen in practice with proper operational controls), the code provides no safeguard against it.

vulnerabilities.title: Missing bridge_watchdog and bridge_pauser Registration in Deployment Creates Operational Gap
vulnerabilities.summary: The deployment scripts (DeployOnL1.s.sol) do not register 'bridge_watchdog' or 'bridge_pauser' named addresses in the AddressManager. Since Bridge uses onlyFromOwnerOrNamed('bridge_watchdog') and _authorizePause checks onlyFromOwnerOrNamed('bridge_pauser'), these resolve to address(0) when not registered. The modifiers allow owner OR the named address, so only the owner (timelock) can perform these actions. This means watchdog and pause operations require governance proposals through the timeloc

description.desc: No register() calls for 'bridge_watchdog' or 'bridge_pauser' in deployment script; owner set to timelock with 7-day delay
Affected function: suspendMessages

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. If bridge_watchdog and bridge_pauser are not registered post-deployment: (1) suspendMessages requires 7-day governance process - cannot quickly suspend suspicious messages during the 1-hour invocation delay on L1; (2) banAddress requires 7-day governance process - cannot quickly ban compromised addresses; (3) Bridge pause/unpause requires 7-day governance process - cannot quickly halt bridge operations during an exploit. This effectively negates the security purpose of the 1-hour invocation delay on L1, which was designed to give watchdogs time to react to suspicious messages.

vulnerabilities.title: USDCAdapter._burnToken ignores return value of USDC.transferFrom and may burn without receiving funds on nonstandard USDC implementations
vulnerabilities.summary: USDCAdapter._burnToken calls `usdc.transferFrom(_from, address(this), _amount); usdc.burn(_amount);` but does not check the boolean return value. If a USDC-like token returns false instead of reverting on failure, the adapter may still call burn, potentially reverting or burning its own balance inconsistently. This can cause bridging/migration burns to fail unpredictably, potentially locking messages or desyncing supply assumptions.

description.desc: unchecked ERC20 boolean return value on transferFrom

vulnerabilities.impact: The defect is a code correctness issue (SWC-104). Impact depends on the USDC implementation: (1) With standard Circle USDC that reverts on failure, the defect is dormant but still represents incorrect code. (2) With a USDC variant returning false, the adapter could burn its own tokens without receiving funds, desyncing bridge accounting. (3) Bridge operations could fail unpredictably, potentially locking cross-chain messages. The defect could become critical if the USDC proxy is upgraded to a version that returns false instead of reverting.

vulnerabilities.title: ERC20Vault onMessageRecalled Token-ETH Coupling Causes Permanent Fund Lock
vulnerabilities.summary: In ERC20Vault.onMessageRecalled, _transferTokens() is called first, then _message.srcOwner.sendEther(_message.value). If the canonical token is paused, blacklisted, or the vault has insufficient balance (due to a separate issue), _transferTokens reverts, preventing the ETH refund as well. The entire recall atomically fails, locking both the user's tokens AND ETH in the bridge/vault with no recovery path. The message status is already RECALLED-in-progress, and the recall cannot be retried.

description.desc: _transferTokens called at line 237 before sendEther at line 238; if _transferTokens reverts, ETH refund is blocked

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. Any user who bridges a pausable/blacklistable canonical ERC20 token alongside ETH value risks having their ETH locked if the token becomes non-transferable. Real-world pausable tokens include USDC, USDT, and many other major tokens. The lock duration is at minimum equal to the token's pause duration, and potentially permanent. The same issue affects ERC721Vault and ERC1155Vault. While retry is possible (mitigating the 'permanent lock' claim somewhat), there is genuinely no mechanism to independently recover the ETH.

vulnerabilities.title: Incomplete Ownership Transfer in USDCAdapter Deployment
vulnerabilities.summary: The deployment script initiates but does not complete the ownership transfer of the USDCAdapter, leaving critical administrative privileges with the deployer EOA.

description.desc: USDCAdapter(adapterProxy).transferOwnership(erc20VaultOwner); without subsequent accept call
Affected function: init

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH - The deployer EOA retains full administrative control over the USDCAdapter including: UUPS upgrade authority (can replace implementation), pause/unpause capability, migration status control (can redirect token flows), and all onlyOwner functions. This is especially critical because USDCAdapter controls USDC bridging, a high-value asset. The other deployment script (DeployOnL1.s.sol) correctly transfers ownership to a timelock, indicating this omission is unintentional.

vulnerabilities.title: Banned Addresses Can Still Receive ETH Refunds
vulnerabilities.summary: The banAddress feature prevents message execution but still allows the banned address to receive ETH if set as the refundTo address.

description.desc: addressBanned check skips invocation but proceeds to refundTo.sendEther

vulnerabilities.impact:
MEDIUM severity. The ban feature is a security control used by the watchdog to isolate compromised or malicious addresses. This bypass means:
1. Banned addresses can continue accumulating ETH through bridge refunds
2. Sanctions enforcement via banning is incomplete
3. Incident response isolation is weakened - a compromised address can still receive bridge funds
4. The bridge value associated with messages targeting banned addresses goes to the banned address as refund rather than being held/frozen

Note: Tokens encoded in message data ARE lost (since invocation is skipped and status is DONE, no recall possible), but ETH value is still delivered to the banned address via refund.

vulnerabilities.title: Guardian Proofs Blocked During Proving Pause Prevents Emergency State Correction
vulnerabilities.summary: TaikoL1.proveBlock() is gated by both whenNotPaused and whenProvingNotPaused modifiers. GuardianProver.approve() calls TaikoL1.proveBlock() when the approval threshold is met. This means guardians—the highest-authority tier intended to be the ultimate arbiter—cannot submit corrective proofs when proving is paused. The protocol has no separate guardian-only proving path, creating a deadlock scenario where pausing prevents the very mechanism needed to resolve the issue that caused the pause.

description.desc: proveBlock has whenProvingNotPaused modifier; no alternative path for guardian-tier proofs
Affected function: approve

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH impact: When proving is paused during an emergency, the protocol enters a state where block verification is completely halted. The guardian tier—the only mechanism capable of resolving proof disputes with ultimate authority—is disabled. This creates a deadlock: the emergency measure (pause) prevents the emergency resolution mechanism (guardian proof) from functioning. All provers' bonds remain locked, L2 chain progression halts from the L1 perspective, and cross-chain state syncing via signal service stops. Recovery requires unpausing which opens a race condition window for malicious actors.

vulnerabilities.title: Proving Window Indefinitely Extended via Repeated Pause/Unpause by chain_pauser
vulnerabilities.summary: The proving window check in _checkProverPermission uses max(ts.timestamp, lastUnpausedAt) + provingWindow*60 >= block.timestamp. Both pauseProving(false) (via LibProving) and TaikoL1.unpause() reset lastUnpausedAt. A chain_pauser who repeatedly toggles provingPaused (true->false) or the contract pause state resets lastUnpausedAt, extending the assigned prover's exclusive proving window indefinitely and preventing any other prover from submitting proofs for first transitions.

description.desc: _checkProverPermission uses max(_ts.timestamp, _state.slotB.lastUnpausedAt) for window calculation; pauseProving(false) resets lastUnpausedAt
Affected function: unpause

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH severity. The attack prevents verification of blocks whose assigned provers collude with chain_pauser. Since verification is sequential (lastVerifiedBlockId can only increase contiguously via LibVerifying.verifyBlocks), blocking one block halts the entire verification chain. All subsequent blocks cannot be verified. Prover bonds are locked. L2 chain finality stalls. Cross-chain state syncing via SignalService stops. The attack costs minimal gas (~100K per toggle cycle) and has no capital requirement beyond gas costs.

vulnerabilities.title: LivenessBond bypass via non-standard ERC20 behavior: LibProposing checks exact balance delta but uses raw IERC20 calls elsewhere
vulnerabilities.summary: LibProposing.proposeBlock enforces tko.balanceOf(this) == tkoBalanceBefore + livenessBond after hooks. However, other code paths use raw IERC20.transfer/transferFrom without SafeERC20 (LibProving, LibVerifying). If TaikoToken is ever upgraded/replaced with a non-standard ERC20 that returns false (instead of reverting) or has transfer hooks that manipulate balances, bond accounting can silently fail (e.g., transfer returns false but function proceeds), breaking bond conservation and potentially c

description.desc: IERC20(tko).transfer(ts.prover, bondToReturn) used without checking return value
Affected function: _overrideWithHigherProof()

vulnerabilities.impact: The unchecked return values create multiple failure modes: provers can lose bonds during verification, contesters can contest for free (transferFrom returning false), and reward distributions can silently fail. While current TaikoToken (OZ ERC20) reverts on failure making this safe today, the code pattern is objectively defective per SWC-104 standards. Any token upgrade or replacement could activate these silent failures, leading to protocol insolvency and stuck funds.

vulnerabilities.title: SGX Instance Self-Replacement Bypasses Key Rotation and Expiry
vulnerabilities.summary: SgxVerifier._replaceInstance does not check if newInstance == oldInstance. An SGX enclave can submit proofs where it replaces itself with the same address, resetting validSince to block.timestamp. This bypasses the 180-day INSTANCE_EXPIRY and the side-channel protection requiring key rotation, allowing a single compromised SGX key to remain valid indefinitely.

description.desc: _replaceInstance sets instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, block.timestamp) without checking newInstance != oldInstance

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH/CRITICAL. The INSTANCE_EXPIRY mechanism is the primary defense against SGX side-channel key compromise. This bug completely negates that protection, allowing a single compromised SGX key to remain valid indefinitely. Combined with the ability to prove arbitrary state transitions (since the compromised key can sign any transition), this could lead to acceptance of invalid L2 state roots, potentially enabling theft of all bridged assets. The contract's own documentation explicitly states keys must be rotated on each proof for side-channel protection.

vulnerabilities.title: Guardian Approval Hash Excludes Proof Data Enabling Unauthorized Liveness Bond Return
vulnerabilities.summary: GuardianProver.approve() computes the approval hash as keccak256(abi.encode(_meta, _tran)), excluding _proof entirely. The last guardian to trigger the threshold determines the actual proof.data passed to TaikoL1.proveBlock. A malicious guardian can include RETURN_LIVENESS_BOND (bytes32) in proof.data to trigger liveness bond return to the assigned prover, even though other guardians did not approve this action.

description.desc: hash = keccak256(abi.encode(_meta, _tran)) - _proof is not included in approval hash
Affected function: proveBlock

vulnerabilities.impact: Each successful attack extracts 250 TKO (the livenessBond) from the TaikoL1 contract to the assignedProver. In cases where the assignedProver should have had their bond slashed (e.g., they failed to prove within the proving window), this represents a direct economic loss to the protocol. Over time, this systematically drains TKO from the protocol treasury, potentially violating the solvency invariant (INV-ECON-001). Additionally, setting blk.livenessBond = 0 means the verification step in LibVerifying won't properly account for the bond in bondToReturn calculations.

vulnerabilities.title: Tier Selection Gaming via Predictable On-Chain Randomness
vulnerabilities.summary: Block minTier is derived from keccak256(block.prevrandao, numBlocks, block.number), which is fully deterministic once the L1 block is known. Proposers can compute the required tier off-chain before submitting. On mainnet, 0.1% of blocks require expensive SGX_ZKVM tier. Proposers can simply skip L1 blocks where the randomness would trigger SGX_ZKVM, waiting for a block where only SGX is needed, systematically avoiding the higher security audit.

description.desc: meta_.difficulty = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(block.prevrandao, b.numBlocks, block.number)); meta_.minTier = ITierProvider(...).getMinTier(uint256(meta_.difficulty))
Affected function: getMinTier

vulnerabilities.impact: The multi-tier proving system is a core security mechanism of the Taiko protocol. The TIER_SGX_ZKVM tier provides a higher level of proof verification (requiring both SGX attestation AND ZKVM proof verification) as a statistical audit of blocks. By gaming the tier selection, proposers can ensure that no blocks ever require ZKVM verification, eliminating this entire security layer. This degrades the protocol's fault detection capability and could allow invalid state transitions proven only at the SGX tier to persist unchallenged by the higher-security verification mechanism. The validity bond for SGX_ZKVM (500 TKO) is also higher than SGX (250 TKO), representing an economic incentive to avoid it.

vulnerabilities.title: Assigned Prover Loses Liveness Bond Despite Proving Correctly When Contested
vulnerabilities.summary: When an assigned prover correctly proves a block but is contested, a higher-tier prover must resolve the dispute. The higher-tier prover becomes ts.prover, permanently locking out the assigned prover (L1_ASSIGNED_PROVER_NOT_ALLOWED). During verification, since ts.prover != assignedProver, the assigned prover loses livenessBond/2 (125 TKO) despite their original proof being correct.

description.desc: if (ts.prover != blk.assignedProver) { bondToReturn -= blk.livenessBond >> 1; } — penalizes assigned prover even when their original proof was correct
Affected function: _checkProverPermission

vulnerabilities.impact: The assigned prover loses livenessBond/2 (125 TKO per mainnet config) despite correctly fulfilling their proving commitment. This creates a perverse incentive: correct provers are punished when their valid proofs are contested. Over time, this could discourage provers from participating, especially if targeted repeatedly. The 4:1 cost ratio (attacker:victim) may be acceptable for motivated attackers seeking to drive specific provers out of the market.

vulnerabilities.title: Arbitrary ERC20 Theft via Unvalidated coinbase in AssignmentHook
vulnerabilities.summary: The `proposeBlock` function allows the proposer to specify an arbitrary `coinbase` address in `BlockParams`. This `coinbase` address is passed to `AssignmentHook.onBlockProposed`, which uses it as the `from` address in `safeTransferFrom` to pay the `proverFee` in ERC20 tokens. A malicious proposer can set `coinbase` to any address that has granted an ERC20 allowance to `AssignmentHook` (such as other legitimate proposers), and set themselves as the `assignedProver`. This allows the attacker to d

description.desc: IERC20(assignment.feeToken).safeTransferFrom(_meta.coinbase, _blk.assignedProver, proverFee);
Affected function: BlockParams

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL. Any address that has approved AssignmentHook for any ERC20 token can have those tokens stolen. In normal protocol operation, proposers who use ERC20 fee tokens must approve AssignmentHook, making them vulnerable. The attack can drain the entire allowance in a single transaction by setting a high proverFee in tierFees.

vulnerabilities.title: SGX Instance Address Re-Registration via Missing addressRegistered Update in _replaceInstance
vulnerabilities.summary: When SgxVerifier.verifyProof replaces an SGX instance via _replaceInstance, the newInstance address is NOT added to the addressRegistered mapping. This allows the same address to be re-registered via registerInstance(), creating a duplicate valid instance ID. An attacker with access to a valid SGX attestation can obtain multiple instance IDs for the same enclave address, bypassing the one-instance-per-address invariant and circumventing the INSTANCE_EXPIRY mechanism by rotating between IDs.

description.desc: _replaceInstance sets instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, ...) but never sets addressRegistered[newInstance] = true; _addInstances at line 195 checks addressRegistered but won't find newInstance

vulnerabilities.impact: The bug allows the same SGX enclave address to hold multiple valid instance IDs. This violates the one-instance-per-address invariant that the addressRegistered mapping is designed to enforce. The practical impacts include: (1) Bypassing INSTANCE_EXPIRY by creating fresh instance IDs for the same key, (2) An enclave could maintain perpetual validity without genuine key rotation, undermining side-channel protection, (3) Multiple valid IDs for one address complicate instance management and deletion by the rollup_watchdog.

vulnerabilities.title: GuardianProver Ownership Not Transferred to Timelock in Deployment Script
vulnerabilities.summary: In the DeployOnL1 script, GuardianProver is initialized with owner=address(0) (defaults to msg.sender), then setGuardians is called, then transferOwnership(timelock) is called. However, since EssentialContract inherits Ownable2StepUpgradeable, transferOwnership only sets pendingOwner — the actual owner remains msg.sender until acceptOwnership is called. The script calls acceptOwnership for sharedAddressManager and rollupAddressManager but NOT for GuardianProver. After deployment, the original de

description.desc: GuardianProver(guardianProver).setGuardians(guardians, minGuardians); GuardianProver(guardianProver).transferOwnership(timelock); — no acceptOwnership call follows for guardianProver
Affected function: setGuardians
Affected function: init

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH — The GuardianProver controls the highest-authority proving mechanism in the Taiko protocol. Guardian-tier proofs can override any lower-tier proof with zero bonds. If the deployer retains ownership and the key is compromised, an attacker could replace all guardians, approve invalid state transitions, and corrupt L2 state integrity. The deployer can also pause guardian proving or upgrade the contract to a malicious implementation.

vulnerabilities.title: SgxVerifier _replaceInstance Accepts Zero Address as New Instance
vulnerabilities.summary: SgxVerifier._replaceInstance does not validate that newInstance is not address(0). While _addInstances explicitly checks for zero address, _replaceInstance unconditionally writes the new instance. If an SGX enclave's proof data contains newInstance = address(0), the instance slot is effectively corrupted — instances[id].addr becomes address(0), and _isInstanceValid will reject it permanently. This instance ID becomes unusable without admin intervention.

description.desc: _replaceInstance writes instances[id] = Instance(newInstance, ...) without checking newInstance != address(0); compare _addInstances line 200: if (_instances[i] == address(0)) revert SGX_INVALID_INSTANCE()

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. A compromised SGX enclave can permanently corrupt its instance slot, reducing SGX proving capacity. Admin intervention (deleteInstances + re-registration) is required for recovery. The defect could also be triggered by a malfunctioning enclave producing malformed proof data. While the attack requires SGX compromise (which would already be serious), the missing validation amplifies the damage by creating a permanent DoS on the instance ID rather than just a one-time invalid proof.

vulnerabilities.title: Permissionless proposer can systematically avoid high-cost proving tiers by waiting for favorable per-block randomness
vulnerabilities.summary: Min-tier selection for each proposed block is derived from L1 block attributes (prevrandao, block.number) and the current numBlocks. A proposer can compute the minTier for the next proposal off-chain after the L1 block is known, and simply skip proposing when the sampled tier is expensive (e.g., SGX+ZKVM), thereby reducing effective security sampling and increasing the chance that only cheaper tiers are used in practice.

description.desc: meta_.difficulty = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(block.prevrandao, b.numBlocks, block.number)); meta_.minTier = ITierProvider(...).getMinTier(uint256(meta_.difficulty));
Affected function: getMinTier(uint256)

vulnerabilities.impact:
The impact is a systematic degradation of the protocol's security model. The random tier selection is designed to ensure that a certain fraction of blocks undergo stronger proof verification (SGX+ZKVM on mainnet, SGX on testnet). By allowing proposers to selectively avoid expensive tiers:
- On mainnet: the intended 0.1% SGX_ZKVM audit rate drops to effectively 0%
- On testnet: the intended 10% SGX audit rate drops to effectively 0%

This means blocks could be verified with only the minimum tier proofs, reducing the overall security guarantee of the rollup. While the guardian tier provides a backstop, the economic security sampling model is undermined.

vulnerabilities.title: Any proposer can drain TaikoL1's accumulated ETH balance via a malicious hook
vulnerabilities.summary: During `proposeBlock`, `TaikoL1` forwards its entire ETH balance to each hook specified by the proposer (`IHook(hook).onBlockProposed{value: address(this).balance}`). If `TaikoL1` has accumulated any ETH (e.g., via selfdestruct or accidental transfers during reentrant calls), a proposer can specify a malicious hook that simply keeps the ETH. The protocol only verifies that the TKO balance increased by the liveness bond, ignoring the stolen ETH.

description.desc: IHook(params.hookCalls[i].hook).onBlockProposed{ value: address(this).balance }(blk, meta_, params.hookCalls[i].data);

vulnerabilities.impact: The financial impact is limited to ETH that has been force-sent to TaikoL1. Under normal operation, TaikoL1 should not accumulate ETH (depositEtherToL2 forwards to bridge). However, selfdestruct can always force-send ETH, and coinbase rewards could accumulate if TaikoL1's address is used as a block coinbase. The defect is real regardless of the typical balance magnitude. This is a code defect because using `address(this).balance` instead of tracking `msg.value` violates the principle of only forwarding the proposer's own funds.

vulnerabilities.title: Unexpected revert propagation can stall verification if SignalService syncChainData reverts inside verifyBlocks finalization
vulnerabilities.summary: verifyBlocks performs external calls to SignalService.getSyncedChainData and potentially syncChainData after updating verifiedTransitionId for multiple blocks and then updating lastVerifiedBlockId at the end. A revert in SignalService calls will revert the whole verification transaction, preventing verification progress and bond returns, potentially halting the chain if SignalService is buggy/misconfigured or becomes temporarily unavailable.

description.desc: _syncChainData calls ISignalService.getSyncedChainData and syncChainData without try/catch; revert aborts verification
Affected function: verifyBlocks(uint64)

vulnerabilities.impact: HIGH impact on protocol liveness: (1) No blocks can be verified while SignalService is malfunctioning. (2) Prover bonds (validityBond + livenessBond) cannot be returned, locking TKO funds. (3) When ring buffer fills after 864,000 unverified blocks, proposeBlock also fails, completely halting the L2 chain. (4) Cross-chain state sync stops, affecting bridge security. Recovery requires admin intervention through the timelock (7-day delay for non-admin-role holders) to fix SignalService configuration.

vulnerabilities.title: Proposer Griefing via Reverting ETH Fee Transfer
vulnerabilities.summary: A malicious prover can DoS block proposers by signing an assignment for ETH payment and then reverting in their fallback function when the `AssignmentHook` attempts to pay them. Because `LibAddress.sendEther` reverts the entire transaction on failure, the proposer's gas is wasted and the block is not proposed.

description.desc: if (!success) revert ETH_TRANSFER_FAILED(); propagates revert to proposer
Affected function: onBlockProposed

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect enables griefing of block proposers by wasting their L1 gas. In a competitive MEV environment, this can be weaponized to monopolize block proposals. The prover has no direct financial cost for this attack beyond initial contract deployment. Impact is bounded by: (1) Proposers can avoid the malicious prover after first failure, (2) Proposers can check if the prover is a contract before using the assignment, (3) The prover still needs to post liveness bond (transferFrom succeeds before sendEther is called). However, note that the liveness bond transferFrom from the prover to TaikoL1 happens BEFORE the ETH payment to the prover, so if the ETH payment reverts, the liveness bond transfer is also reverted, meaning the prover loses nothing.

vulnerabilities.title: ETH Deposit Fee Deposit Re-Processed with Fee Deduction - Systematic Proposer Fee Leakage
vulnerabilities.summary: In LibDepositing.processDeposits(), the accumulated totalFee is written as a new deposit entry in the ETH deposit ring buffer at index numEthDeposits. This fee deposit is treated identically to a user deposit and will be processed by the NEXT block's processDeposits call, where a processing fee is deducted from it. This means the original proposer (fee recipient) receives less than the full fee on L2, with the difference going to the next block's proposer as processing revenue.

description.desc: Fee deposit written to ethDeposits[numEthDeposits % ringBufferSize] with numEthDeposits++; this entry falls within the processable range for next block, causing recursive fee deduction

vulnerabilities.impact: Every proposer who processes ETH deposits and earns a fee deposit will have that fee reduced by a processing fee when their fee deposit is eventually processed. The percentage loss depends on basefee conditions: at 50 Gwei with 8 deposits, ~12.5% loss; with 32 deposits, ~3.1% loss (since totalFee is larger relative to the fixed per-deposit fee). This is a permanent, systematic issue affecting all proposers across the protocol's lifetime.

vulnerabilities.title: SGX Instance Duplication via Missing Registration State Update
vulnerabilities.summary: When an SGX instance rotates its key, the new instance address is not marked as registered. This allows the enclave to re-register the same new address via the attestation flow, obtaining multiple valid instance IDs for a single key.

description.desc: Missing addressRegistered[newInstance] = true; in _replaceInstance

vulnerabilities.impact: The vulnerability allows an SGX enclave to obtain multiple valid instance IDs for a single key, breaking the one-to-one invariant between addresses and instance IDs. This enables: (1) bypassing the 180-day expiry by maintaining multiple IDs and re-registering as needed, (2) maintaining proof capability even if one instance ID is deleted by the rollup_watchdog, and (3) potential corruption of SGX instance registry integrity relied upon by off-chain monitoring systems.

vulnerabilities.title: ERC20Airdrop Initialization Deploys with Zero Config — Claims Blocked Until Manual Setup
vulnerabilities.summary: The ERC20Airdrop deployment script initializes with `ERC20Airdrop.init(address(0), 0, 0, bytes32(0), bridgedTko, vaultAddress)` — claimStart=0, claimEnd=0, merkleRoot=0. The `ongoingClaim` modifier blocks all claims when merkleRoot == 0 or claimStart == 0 or claimEnd == 0. Claims require a subsequent setConfig() call. If the owner fails to call setConfig or misconfigures it, no users can claim. Additionally, using transferFrom without SafeERC20 for the token transfer could cause silent failures

description.desc: IERC20(token).transferFrom(vault, user, amount) — uses raw transferFrom not SafeERC20.safeTransferFrom; combined with zero-config deployment

vulnerabilities.impact: Medium severity. The zero-config deployment creates an operational window where claims are impossible (by design, easily fixable via setConfig). The transferFrom without SafeERC20 is a genuine code defect that could cause permanent loss of airdrop claims if the token behaves non-standardly. Given that bridgedTko is expected to be a standard TaikoToken, the practical risk is low, but the code defect exists.

vulnerabilities.title: Gas Griefing via ES256Verifier Panic Halts Proving
vulnerabilities.summary: The `verifyES256Signature` function uses `assert(success)` after a `staticcall` to the external `ES256VERIFIER` (P256 precompile). If the precompile fails (e.g., due to an invalid public key not on the curve), `success` is false. The `assert(false)` triggers a Panic (opcode 0xFE), which consumes 100% of the transaction's allocated gas. An attacker can submit a malformed SGX quote that causes this panic, forcing the prover who attempts to verify the block to lose their entire gas limit.

description.desc: (bool success, bytes memory ret) = ES256VERIFIER.staticcall(args); assert(success);

vulnerabilities.impact: LOW-MEDIUM severity, not HIGH as claimed. The code defect exists (assert on external call result instead of require/conditional return), but the impact is: (1) If ES256VERIFIER is unavailable, attestation reverts instead of returning false - a graceful degradation failure. (2) Gas is NOT consumed at 100% because Solidity 0.8.24 uses REVERT for assert, refunding remaining gas. (3) The deployed P256Verifier is designed to never revert, making this a defense-in-depth issue rather than an active attack vector. The hypothesis overstates impact by approximately 10-100x.

vulnerabilities.title: Strict Sequential txId Enforcement Causes Cross-Chain Head-of-Line Blocking
vulnerabilities.summary: In `CrossChainOwned`, `onMessageInvocation` enforces strict sequential execution via `if (txId != nextTxId) revert`. If the inner `address(this).call(txdata)` reverts, the entire transaction reverts, and `nextTxId` is not incremented. The bridge message remains FAILED/RETRIABLE. If the transaction cannot be made to succeed (e.g., it attempts to set a config that is no longer valid due to external state changes), `nextTxId` is permanently stuck. All subsequent cross-chain admin commands will fail

description.desc: if (!success) revert XCO_TX_REVERTED(); emit TransactionExecuted(nextTxId++, bytes4(txdata));

vulnerabilities.impact: CRITICAL for governance availability. A permanently stuck nextTxId means: (1) No more cross-chain admin operations can execute, (2) The admin cannot pause the contract during an active exploit, (3) The admin cannot upgrade the contract to fix the issue (circular dependency if upgrades go through the same cross-chain channel), (4) The admin cannot change EIP-1559 configurations, (5) The admin cannot withdraw funds from the contract. This effectively bricks the cross-chain ownership mechanism permanently unless there is a separate local owner or an alternative upgrade path not visible in the provided code.

vulnerabilities.title: Forced Airdrop Claim via Unvalidated Delegation Signature
vulnerabilities.summary: In `ERC20Airdrop.claimAndDelegate`, the `delegationData` parameter is passed directly to `IVotes(token).delegateBySig`. The contract does not verify that the recovered signer of the delegation signature matches the `user` receiving the airdrop. An attacker can call this function for any eligible user, providing a valid delegation signature signed by the *attacker*. This forces the user to receive the airdrop tokens immediately, while delegating the attacker's (likely zero) votes.

description.desc: IVotes(token).delegateBySig(delegatee, nonce, expiry, v, r, s); // without checking signer == user

vulnerabilities.impact: Minimal impact. Forced claiming is a design pattern used across all airdrop contracts (ERC20Airdrop2, ERC721Airdrop). The delegation aspect is a no-op when called with a mismatched signature because delegateBySig delegates the signer's power, not the user's. Users can still delegate their voting power manually after receiving tokens.

vulnerabilities.title: AutomataDcapV3Attestation._checkTcbLevels Returns True for Unrecognized TCB Status
vulnerabilities.summary: The _checkTcbLevels function returns (true, TCBStatus.TCB_UNRECOGNIZED) when no TCB level matches the PCK certificate's SVN values. The boolean 'true' is incorrect — an unrecognized TCB should not be marked as 'verified'. While _attestationTcbIsValid currently filters out TCB_UNRECOGNIZED, this is a demonstrable code defect where the intermediate verification result is wrong. Any future code path that relies on the boolean return without also checking the status enum would accept unrecognized (p

description.desc: Function returns (true, TCBInfoStruct.TCBStatus.TCB_UNRECOGNIZED) at line 206 when no TCB level matches; the boolean should be false for unrecognized status

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity code defect. The boolean return contradicts the semantic meaning of TCB_UNRECOGNIZED. Currently mitigated by _attestationTcbIsValid, but the wrong intermediate result wastes gas (~1.3M for cert chain + sig verification), returns different retData than expected for failures, and creates a latent vulnerability for any derived contract that trusts the boolean return.

vulnerabilities.title: SignalService Cannot Be Paused Leaving No Emergency Stop During Active Exploit
vulnerabilities.summary: SignalService._authorizePause always reverts with SS_UNSUPPORTED, making the pause() and unpause() functions permanently inoperative. If a vulnerability is discovered in Merkle proof verification (LibTrieProof, MerkleTrie) or in the signal storage mechanism, there is no way to halt SignalService operations. Since Bridge._proveSignalReceived depends on SignalService for proof verification, all bridge operations continue even during an active exploit of the proof system.

description.desc: function _authorizePause(address) internal pure override { revert SS_UNSUPPORTED(); }

vulnerabilities.impact: No actual impact from a code defect perspective. The SignalService's inability to be paused is an intentional design choice. The Bridge provides the actual emergency stop mechanism. The system has multiple layered defenses: Bridge pause, message suspension, address banning, authorization revocation, and UUPS upgrades.

vulnerabilities.title: Unchecked Invocation Delay Addition Can Overflow to Bypass Security Delay
vulnerabilities.summary: In processMessage, when msg.sender is not the preferredExecutor, the invocationDelay is increased by invocationExtraDelay inside an unchecked block: `unchecked { invocationDelay += invocationExtraDelay; }`. While current hardcoded values (3600 + 384) are safe, getInvocationDelays() is a virtual function that can be overridden in derived contracts. If a derived Bridge contract returns large delay values, the unchecked addition wraps around to a small value, effectively eliminating the security de

description.desc: unchecked { invocationDelay += invocationExtraDelay; } - uint256 addition in unchecked block

vulnerabilities.impact: If exploited in a derived contract, the security delay window (designed to give watchdogs time to suspend malicious messages on L1) is completely bypassed. This could allow immediate processing of messages that should be subject to a 1-hour+ delay, removing the time window for detecting and stopping fraudulent cross-chain messages. The impact is MEDIUM because: it requires a derived contract with incorrectly large delay values (unlikely but possible due to the virtual design pattern), and current deployments use the base Bridge contract with safe hardcoded values.

vulnerabilities.title: Unauthorized Chain Data Caching via Direct proveSignalReceived Calls Bypasses syncChainData Authorization
vulnerabilities.summary: SignalService.syncChainData requires isAuthorized[msg.sender]==true. However, proveSignalReceived is public and calls _cacheChainData internally, which calls _syncChainData. This bypasses the authorization check, allowing any caller to write chain data (state roots, signal roots) and advance topBlockId for any chain. While the data must be validated by Merkle proofs, the authorization restriction on syncChainData is rendered meaningless for data that can be proven via multi-hop proofs.

description.desc: proveSignalReceived calls _cacheChainData → _syncChainData without isAuthorized check; syncChainData requires isAuthorized

vulnerabilities.impact:
Medium impact:
1. topBlockId advancement is permanent (monotonic) - once advanced, it cannot be rolled back
2. getSyncedChainData with blockId=0 returns data at the topBlockId, which may now point to a different block than what the authorized syncer intended
3. Additional chain data entries are written to storage (storage griefing)
4. The data itself is cryptographically valid (proven via Merkle proofs), limiting the ability to write malicious data
5. The authorization model on syncChainData is fundamentally undermined for any data accessible via proofs

vulnerabilities.title: Fee-on-Transfer Token Accounting Mismatch Drains ERC20Vault on Destination Chain
vulnerabilities.summary: ERC20Vault._handleMessage on the source chain correctly accounts for fee-on-transfer tokens via balance delta measurement. However, _transferTokens on the destination chain calls `IERC20(token_).safeTransfer(_to, _amount)` with the full recorded amount. If the canonical token on the destination chain has transfer fees, the recipient receives less than _amount but the vault's accounting considers the full amount transferred. Over multiple bridge cycles, the vault's canonical token balance becomes

description.desc: IERC20(token_).safeTransfer(_to, _amount) transfers full amount without measuring actual balance change on destination

vulnerabilities.impact: LOW-MEDIUM impact. The code defect is real (asymmetric FoT handling) but the specific impact claim (vault insolvency) is incorrect for standard fee-on-transfer implementations. Actual impact: (1) Recipients silently receive fewer tokens than the protocol reports, (2) TokenReceived events emit incorrect amounts, (3) User funds are partially lost to uncommunicated fees on withdrawal, (4) For exotic fee models where sender is charged extra beyond the transfer amount, withdrawals could revert causing DoS.

vulnerabilities.title: Liveness Bond Bypass via Custom Malicious Hook Circumventing AssignmentHook
vulnerabilities.summary: LibProposing.proposeBlock only checks that TKO balance increased by livenessBond after hooks execute. It does NOT verify that the livenessBond came from the assignedProver's approval. A proposer can deploy a custom hook that transfers livenessBond TKO from any source (including the proposer's own balance), effectively proposing blocks with an assigned prover who never consented or staked.

description.desc: if (tko.balanceOf(address(this)) != tkoBalance + _config.livenessBond) revert — only checks balance delta, not source

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect enables: (1) Griefing specific provers by naming them as assignedProver without consent, locking them into a proving obligation they didn't agree to. (2) During the exclusive proving window, only the named (non-consenting) prover can submit the first transition proof. (3) The proving market's economic model breaks down because assigned provers don't actually consent via signature or stake their own bonds. (4) The attacker bears cost of 250 TKO livenessBond per block (and may lose half if someone else proves), so this is primarily a griefing vector rather than a profit mechanism.

vulnerabilities.title: ETH deposit queue can be permanently blocked by repeatedly forcing near-full ring buffer occupancy
vulnerabilities.summary: ETH deposits are accepted only if pendingDeposits < ringBufferSize-1. An attacker can spam deposits (each at least 1 ETH per config) until the queue approaches capacity, causing subsequent users to be unable to deposit. Because clearing the queue depends on block proposals (processDeposits) and is limited per block (maxCountPerBlock), a sustained attacker can keep the queue near capacity, effectively DoS-ing deposits for extended periods.

description.desc: return ... && (numEthDeposits - nextEthDepositToProcess) < ethDepositRingBufferSize - 1;
Affected function: depositEtherToL2(address _recipient)

vulnerabilities.impact: No code defect exists. The hypothesis describes the inherent capacity limitation of any bounded buffer. The design includes appropriate economic barriers (1 ETH minimum deposit) and drain mechanisms (32 deposits processed per proposal). The 'permanently blocked' claim is false since processDeposits continuously frees capacity. At worst, this is a temporary griefing that costs the attacker significantly more than it costs victims (who merely experience delayed deposits).

vulnerabilities.title: SgxVerifier.deleteInstances Leaves addressRegistered Permanently Blocking Re-Registration
vulnerabilities.summary: When deleteInstances removes an SGX instance, it deletes instances[idx] but does NOT clear addressRegistered[instances[idx].addr]. This leaves the flag permanently set to true, preventing the same address from ever being re-registered via either addInstances (onlyOwner) or registerInstance (attestation path). Combined with _replaceInstance not setting addressRegistered for new addresses, this creates asymmetric state: deleted old addresses are permanently blocked, while replacement new addresses

description.desc: delete instances[idx] without clearing addressRegistered[instances[idx].addr]; _addInstances checks if (addressRegistered[_instances[i]]) revert SGX_ALREADY_ATTESTED()

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity. The defect causes permanent state pollution in the SGX instance registry. Operationally: (1) Deleted enclave addresses can never be re-registered, forcing creation of new enclave keys even when re-using existing ones would be safe. (2) Replacement addresses bypass uniqueness checks, allowing potential duplicate instance IDs for the same address, which could be exploited to maintain perpetually valid instance IDs by alternating between them. Over the protocol's lifetime, this accumulates as more instances are deleted or replaced.

vulnerabilities.title: LibProving._createTransition Unchecked nextTransitionId Overflow Corrupts Transition State
vulnerabilities.summary: In _createTransition, nextTransitionId is incremented in an unchecked block: `tid_ = _blk.nextTransitionId++`. Being a uint32, after 2^32 increments it overflows to 0. Transition ID 0 is the uninitialized state, and overflowing would cause new transitions to map to ID 0, colliding with the reserved sentinel value. While the comment states this is unrealistic, each transition requires a unique parentHash and a separate proveBlock call, making griefing expensive but theoretically possible over ext

description.desc: unchecked { tid_ = _blk.nextTransitionId++; } with uint32 type allowing wraparound to 0

vulnerabilities.impact: MEDIUM severity code defect. The unchecked uint32 overflow creates a theoretically reachable invalid state where a block becomes permanently unverifiable. In the worst case, if the affected block is in the verification chain (between lastVerifiedBlockId and numBlocks), chain verification would permanently stall at that block. Practical exploitability is extremely low due to the ~4 billion transactions required.
